rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE RESTATED SANDRA A. HUGGINS 1989 TRUST u/d/t September 12, 1989
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR The Purpose Of Posting Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 3] As the appellate court explained in Sayta v. Chu (2017) 17 Cal. App. 5th 960, "Wackeen does not stand for the proposition that parties may confer jurisdiction on trial courts by including language in a settlement agreement but not asking the court to retain jurisdiction. [citating Hagan Eng'g, Inc. v. Mills (2003) 115 Cal. App. 4th 1004, 1010]. "[S]ettlement language purporting to vest the trial court with retained jurisdiction after the dismissal [is] a nullity: Subject matter jurisdiction cannot be conferred by consent, waiver or estoppel." [citing Hagan, supra, at p. 1008.]" (Sayta v. Chu, supra, at 967, emphasis in original.) The parties in Sayta, like the parties in this matter, "made their Agreement confidential, and nothing in the record indicates any part of it was communicated to the trial court before the dismissals." (Id. at 967.) The Sayta court concluded, "The parties were required to present to the trial court a proper request to retain jurisdiction for purpose of section 664.6 motions. [citation.] [Appellant] fails to explain how the court could have fathomed a "request" for retained jurisdiction, much less granted it sub silentio from a secret handshake of the parties." (Ibid.) Movant relies heavily on Tarrant Bell Prop., LLC v. Superior Ct. (2011) 51 Cal.4th 538 in the Reply. Tarrant Bell Prop., LLC does not discuss section 664.6 and makes no ruling regarding a trial court's retention of jurisdiction under section 664.6. That case concerns a trial court's discretion to refuse to compel arbitration or appoint a referee under Code of Civil Procedure section 638. Movant's summary and characterization of the case's holding and legal significance is incorrect. In light of the court's lack of continuing jurisdiction over this matter, the court will not address the competing substantive arguments presented in the papers. The court's ruling on this Motion is not intended to affect any potential or pending related civil claims or action(s) (see, e.g., Wackeen v. Malis (2002) 97 Cal. App. 4th 429, 433, "The dismissal of this suit by Wackeen did not adversely affect the settlement agreement itself or Moran's right to have it enforced."). Trustee shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE RESTATED SANDRA A. HUGGINS 1989 TRUST u/d/t September 12, 1989
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 3] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Before the court is Petitioner Mark McMechen's Motion to Enforce Settlement Agreement. Respondent Trustee Faye Taam's requests to strike or ignore portions of the moving papers are DENIED. The Motion to Enforce Settlement Agreement is DENIED. This court lacks jurisdiction to hear the Motion to Enforce Settlement Agreement under Code of Civil procedure section 664.6. Background: On April 3, 2024, a Notice of Settlement Conference was entered in this matter, setting Settlement Conference for April 24, 2024. On May 7, 2024, the matter was removed from the Settlement Conference Calendar. The court record states the settlement conference was held and the matter did not settle. On May 13, 2024, Petitioners Bonnie McMechen and Mark McMechen, through counsel, filed Request for Dismissal asking the court to dismiss "entire action of all parties and all causes of action" with prejudice. On June 4, 2024, Petitioners Bonnie McMechen and Mark McMechen, through counsel, filed Request for Dismissal asking the court to dismiss "entire action of all parties and all causes of action" with prejudice. On June 6, 2024, the trial date set for June 10, 2024 was vacated, based on the request for dismissal filed June 4, 2024. The instant Motion was filed May 15, 2025. The undated Settlement Agreement is included as Exhibit A to Movant's declaration in support of the Motion. The Settlement Agreement was apparently signed by all parties in May 2024. Discussion: When there is a voluntary dismissal of an entire action, and no request for the trial court to retain jurisdiction under section 664.6, the court's jurisdiction over the parties and the subject matter terminates. (Wackeen v. Malis (2002) 97 Cal. App. 4th 429, 437; Hagan Eng'g, Inc. v. Mills (2003) 115 Cal. App. 4th 1004, 1008; Eagle Fire & Water Restoration, Inc. v. City of Dinuba (2024) 102 Cal. App. 5th 448, 458-460.) Movant previously voluntarily dismissed the underlying petition, without any party expressly requesting orally or in writing that the court retain jurisdiction to potentially enforce settlement agreements. This court lacks jurisdiction to hear the Motion requesting enforcement of the Settlement Agreement under Code of Civil procedure section 664.6.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE RESTATED SANDRA A. HUGGINS 1989 TRUST u/d/t September 12, 1989
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR The Purpose Of Posting Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 3] As stated in case law, "A request for the trial court to retain jurisdiction under section 664.6 'must conform to the same three requirements which the Legislature and the courts have deemed necessary for section 664.6 enforcement of the settlement itself: the request must be made (1) during the pendency of the case, not after the case has been dismissed in its entirety, (2) by the parties themselves, and (3) either in a writing signed by the parties or orally before the court.'" (Mesa RHF Partners, L.P. v. City of Los Angeles (2019) 33 Cal. App. 5th 913, 917, quoting Wackeen v. Malis (2002) 97 Cal.App.4th 429, 440.) As discussed in Mesa RHF Partners, L.P. v. City of Los Angeles (2019) 33 Cal. App. 5th 913, parties may invoke section 664.6 and properly request the trial court retain jurisdiction over a matter by filing either (1) a stipulation and proposed order attaching a copy of the settlement agreement and requesting that the trial court retain jurisdiction under section 664.6, or (2) a stipulation and proposed order signed by the parties noting the settlement and requesting that the trial court retain jurisdiction under section 664.6. "The process need not be complex. But strict compliance demands that the process be followed." (Id. at 918.) Parties in this matter did not comply with the process described in Mesa RHF Partners, L.P. A stipulation and proposed order requesting the court retain jurisdiction under section 664.6 was not filed prior to Movant's dismissal of the entire action with prejudice. Upon review of the Minutes in this matter, no party noted the Settlement Agreement at a hearing on this matter or made an oral request that the court retain jurisdiction under section 664.6, prior to Movant's dismissal of the entire action with prejudice. This Motion is the first time the Settlement Agreement has been presented to the court. Movant's argument that paragraph 2.8 of the written Settlement Agreement meets the requirements to request the trial court retain jurisdiction under section 664.6 exhibits the same legal fallacies discussed and rejected in cited case law. Movant argues Wackeen v. Malis (2002) 97 Cal.App.4th 429 stands for the legal principle that the parties' written agreement requesting jurisdiction be retained under section 664.6 satisfies alone the requirements for the trial court to retain jurisdiction. This argument is incorrect, as the Wackeen court held the opposite: "[W]e so hold, that even though a settlement may call for a case to be dismissed, or the plaintiff may dismiss the suit of its own accord, the court may nevertheless retain jurisdiction to enforce the terms of the settlement, until such time as all of its terms have been performed by the parties, if the parties have requested this specific retention of jurisdiction. (Id. at 439, emphasis in original). The Wackeen court concluded, "We reject [appellant]'s assertions that various provisions in the settlement agreement show that the parties intended, or operate as a request for, retention of jurisdiction. None of them are the clear express request for retention of jurisdiction that we hold is necessary to permit the court to retain jurisdiction to summarily enforce, under section 664.6, the provisions of a settlement agreement after a case has been dismissed." (Id. at 441.)
